skill.name: mcp-integration
skill.description: Guides developers through configuring MCP servers in Claude Code plugins, including stdio, SSE, HTTP, WebSocket, authentication, tool naming, and testing.
skill.summary: Configure MCP servers for Claude Code plugins with secure authentication and tool access patterns.
skill.author: anthropics

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported a critical combination of network access, credential variables, and command execution. Manual review found documentation and example configuration rather than hidden executable behavior or prompt injection, but MCP integrations can run local servers and connect to remote services. Publish with warnings about trusted endpoints, least-privilege tool permissions, and secret handling.

medium_findings.title: Stdio MCP Examples Launch Local Processes
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE for operational risk, not malicious code. The skill documents stdio MCP configurations that run local commands such as npx, Python modules, or plugin-root executables. This is expected for MCP, but unsafe command paths or untrusted servers could execute code. Confidence: 0.84. Confidence reasoning: Multiple example configurations show command and args fields used to spawn local MCP servers, while the files are documentation and JSON examples rather than executable payloads.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Multiple example configurations show command and args fields used to spawn local MCP servers. The context is instructional documentation, so the risk is real but not evidence of malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Examples Pass Secrets Through Environment Variables
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE for sensitive configuration guidance, with safe intent. Examples reference API keys, database URLs, bearer tokens, and API secrets through environment variables. This is the recommended pattern, but users could expose secrets if they connect to untrusted servers or commit real values. Confidence: 0.88. Confidence reasoning: The flagged lines explicitly contain credential variable placeholders and surrounding text tells users not to hardcode or commit secrets.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence directly shows placeholders for database URLs and API keys. Context confirms these are examples and includes advice to avoid hardcoded credentials.

medium_findings.title: Remote MCP Endpoint Configuration Can Send Data Off Host
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE for network capability, not exfiltration. The skill shows HTTP, SSE, and WebSocket URLs for hosted MCP servers and token headers. Remote MCP integrations can transmit prompts, tool inputs, and credentials to configured services, so users must trust endpoint ownership. Confidence: 0.82. Confidence reasoning: The examples contain concrete remote URL fields and authentication headers, but no code secretly sends data to an undisclosed destination.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Remote endpoint examples and auth headers are explicit. The endpoints are examples or known service URLs, so this is capability risk rather than confirmed data exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Documentation Mentions Loading .env Files
medium_findings.description: NEEDS REVIEW for user safety. The authentication guide shows a documentation template that can load variables from a .env file. It also says to add the file to .gitignore, so the intent is safe, but users should avoid broad shell exports from untrusted files. Confidence: 0.64. Confidence reasoning: The .env guidance is real and security-sensitive, but it appears inside a README template with protective guidance rather than executable code.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The .env loading advice is security-sensitive but appears in documentation. The same section advises adding the file to .gitignore, reducing evidence of harmful intent.

low_findings.title: Command Execution Alerts Are Mostly Markdown Fence False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for scanner-reported Ruby or shell backtick execution. The many backticks are Markdown code fences and inline literals used to explain JSON, commands, and tool names. No evidence found that these files execute shell code themselves. Confidence: 0.93. Confidence reasoning: Representative flagged lines are inside Markdown examples, and this skill contains no script file that would execute those snippets automatically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Representative flagged lines are Markdown fences and inline examples. There are no executable source files that run these examples automatically.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Documentation Context
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for confirmed weak cryptography. The flagged tool-usage lines describe MCP tool schemas, allowed-tools frontmatter, or example task data, not cryptographic operations. One HMAC example uses SHA-256 for request signing guidance, not a weak algorithm. Confidence: 0.86. Confidence reasoning: The cited weak-crypto locations do not contain active cryptographic code, and no insecure hashing implementation was found in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static hits are documentation text or a SHA-256 HMAC example. No active weak cryptographic implementation is present in the skill files.

content.value_statement: Plugin developers need reliable MCP setup guidance for external tools and services. This skill explains server types, authentication, tool naming, and testing steps for Claude Code plugins.
actual_capabilities: Explains .mcp.json and inline plugin.json MCP configuration methods.
Compares stdio, SSE, HTTP, and WebSocket MCP server types.
Documents OAuth, bearer token, API key, and environment variable authentication patterns.
Shows how MCP tool names are generated and referenced in commands.
Provides testing, debugging, and troubleshooting checklists for MCP integrations.
limitations: Does not implement an MCP server or install dependencies.
Does not verify that a remote MCP endpoint is trustworthy.
Requires developers to supply their own credentials and service URLs.
Examples must be adapted before use in production plugins.

use_cases.title: Add hosted service tools
use_cases.description: Configure a plugin to connect Claude Code to a hosted MCP service with OAuth or token authentication.

use_cases.title: Package local developer tools
use_cases.description: Bundle a stdio MCP server with a plugin and document the required command, paths, and environment variables.

use_cases.title: Audit MCP command access
use_cases.description: Review which MCP tools a command can use and replace broad wildcards with specific tool names.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a server type
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning a first MCP integration.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose the right MCP server type for my Claude Code plugin. My service is [local or hosted], uses [auth method], and needs [tools].

prompt_templates.title: Draft MCP configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an MCP configuration plan for a Claude Code plugin that connects to [service]. Include server type, required environment variables, and tool naming notes.

prompt_templates.title: Review authentication setup
prompt_templates.scenario: Developer validating token or OAuth handling.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my MCP authentication approach for [service]. Check token storage, environment variables, scopes, and setup documentation.

prompt_templates.title: Harden a multi-server plugin
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review before publishing a plugin.
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this multi-server MCP plugin design. Identify risky command execution, broad tool permissions, credential exposure, and insecure network endpoints.

output_examples.input: I need to connect a Claude Code plugin to a hosted task service.
output: Recommendation: use an SSE or HTTP MCP server with OAuth if the service supports it.
Document required scopes and verify the endpoint uses HTTPS.
Pre-allow only the specific task tools needed by each command.

output_examples.input: My plugin needs a local database MCP server.
output: Recommendation: use a stdio server and reference paths through the plugin root variable.
Pass database settings through environment variables instead of committed files.
Test startup with debug logs and document required variables in the README.

output_examples.input: Can I allow every MCP tool from my server?
output_examples.output: Use specific tool names when possible. Wildcards increase the chance that a command can call tools outside its intended workflow.

best_practices: Use HTTPS or WSS for every remote MCP endpoint.
Store tokens in environment variables or OAuth storage, not plugin files.
Pre-allow only the MCP tools each command actually needs.
anti_patterns: Hardcoding API tokens, passwords, or tenant identifiers in plugin configuration.
Using wildcard MCP tool permissions for narrow commands.
Running user-provided command paths through stdio MCP configuration.

faq.question: Does this skill create an MCP server?
faq.answer: No. It provides configuration and integration guidance for MCP servers used by Claude Code plugins.

faq.question: Which MCP server types are covered?
faq.answer: It covers stdio, SSE, HTTP, and WebSocket server types.

faq.question: How should credentials be handled?
faq.answer: Use OAuth when available. Otherwise, pass secrets through environment variables and document required setup.

faq.question: Can commands use MCP tools automatically?
faq.answer: Commands should list needed MCP tools in allowed-tools. Specific tool names are safer than wildcards.

faq.question: Does this skill validate endpoint security?
faq.answer: No. Developers must verify remote endpoint ownership, transport security, and token scope requirements.

faq.question: Is it compatible with Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The marketplace metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.
